concepts.title: Thermohaline Circulation
concepts.description: It is the movement of deep ocean water due to changes in temperature and salinity, which plays a crucial role in distributing heat around the globe.

concepts.title: Surface Currents
concepts.description: Surface currents are driven by wind patterns, tides, and the Coriolis force, moving water from high to low-pressure areas.

concepts.title: Equatorial Currents
concepts.description: Equatorial currents are the ocean currents that flow along the equator, driven by trade winds and the Coriolis force, resulting in a net flow from the west to the east.

concepts.title: Wind-Driven Currents
concepts.description: Mnemonics: 'WIND' - Wind causes ocean water to move from high to low-pressure areas. 'I' - Increased wind friction generates more currents. 'N' - North-south winds produce coastal currents. 'D' - Deep ocean currents are driven by wind patterns.

concepts.title: Tidal Currents
concepts.description: Students often confuse tidal currents with wind-driven currents. However, tidal currents are caused by gravitational forces of the moon and sun, while wind-driven currents are driven by wind patterns.

concepts.title: Types of Ocean Currents
concepts.description: There are three types of ocean currents: surface currents, deep-water currents, and thermohaline circulation. Understanding the differences between these currents is essential for the exam.

concepts.title: Longshore Currents
concepts.description: Longshore currents are the ocean currents that flow parallel to the coastline, driven by the Coriolis force and the shape of the coastline.

concepts.title: Cold and Warm Ocean Currents
concepts.description: Cold ocean currents are found near the poles, while warm ocean currents are found near the equator. The interaction between these currents affects climate and ecosystems.
